content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance Administration          Executive Legal          Enforcement          Conflict of Interest
916: 322-5660                                322-5901              322-6441            322-6441

August 22, 1977

Pauline Kearny
6025 Linda Lee Drive
Yucca Valley, CA 92284

Dear Ms. Kearney:

Your letter to the Secretary of State's Office concerning your
filing obligations under the Political Reform Act has been referred
to me for response.

As Mr. Maness of the Secretary of State's Office stated in his letter
to you on August 4, there is no prohibition under the Political Reform
Act concerning selling or raffling donated items. There is, however,
a reporting obligation. The item must be reported when it is received
by the Committee. In this case, the fair market value of the quilt or
afgan must be reported as an in-kind (non-monetary) contribution to
your campaign committee. Any tickets sold to the fund raiser must be
reported as contributions.

I trust this answers your question. If I may be of additional assis-
tance, please do not hesitate to let me know.

I am enclosing some fact sheets for your use which I think you might
find helpful, especially the sheet outlining recommended recordkeeping
guidelines.

Vary truly yours,

Regina Siciliano-Kutchins
Technical Assistance
Program Coordinator

RSK/gl

Enclosure

August 4, 1977


Ms. Pauline Kearney
Committee for Citizens for Self Government
6025 Linda Lee Drive
Yucca Valley, California 92284

Dear Ms. Kearney:

     In response to your letter dated July 11, 1977, I have
found no prohibition in the Elections Code or Political Reform
Act against a political committee receiving donated articles
for use in fund-raising raffles or sales.

     The major question appears to be how the donation and the
money received for selling or raffling the donated item will
be reported by your political committee under the Political
Reform Act of 1974, which I understand that you have a copy of.
There is the possibility that they may be counted to some extent
as contributions to the committee under Government Code Section
82015 and associated regulations. I suggest that you contact the
Fair Political Practices Commission at P.O. Box 807, Sacramento,
California 95804, as the agency responsible for administration
and implementation of the Political Reform Act, for a detailed
answer. I have forwarded a copy of your letter for their infor-
mation.

                                          Very truly yours,

                                          WILLIAM N. DURLEY
                                          Assistant to the Secretary of State
                                          Elections and Political Reform


                                          RICHARD B. MANESS
                                          Legal Counsel
                                          Elections and Political Reform

RBM:gb

cc: Fair Political Practices Commission

Committee for Citizens for Self-Government
6025 Linda Lee Drive

YUCCA VALLEY, CALIFORNIA 92284


July 11, 1977

Kevin P. Monroe, Auditor
Political Reform Division
111 Capitol Mall, Room 200
P.O. Box 1467
Sacramento, Ca. 95807

Dear Mr. Monroe:

Thank you for your patience in answering my many questions by letter and telephone. I ask your indulgence again for the following question:

Several people have offered to donate afghans or quilts for us to raffle off - sell - or whatever we may do according to law. We would like to sell tickets and have the tickets read: "Donation".

May we use the raffle system by selling tickets and then drawing the attachment to the ticket that would have a number on it or address of the purchaser? The Committee did not know whether the "raffle" system is legal for us to use. If this question comes under another department would you please answer as far as your department is concerned, and also have the other department write to us so that I may have it in writing; We will, of course, report any donations on the forms to be filed with your department.

Also, would you please send me:

1. Copy of the amendments of the Political Reform Act which gives legislation effective January 1, 1977.(Dated Dec.8, 1976)

2. State of California Information Manual on Campaign Disclosure Provisions of the Political Reform Act for 1976.

Thank you for your kind attention to my request.

Very truly yours,

Pauline
Pauline Kearney,
Volunteer Secretary

sections.conclusion: There is no prohibition under the Political Reform Act concerning selling or raffling donated items. There is, however, a reporting obligation. The item must be reported when it is received by the Committee. In this case, the fair market value of the quilt or item must be reported as an in-kind (non-monetary) contribution to your campaign committee. Any tickets sold to the fund raiser must be reported as contributions.
sections.question_synthetic: May a political committee use a raffle or ticket system to sell donated items (afghans/quilts) as a fundraising mechanism, and how should such donations and proceeds be reported under the Political Reform Act?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The Political Reform Act does not prohibit raffling donated items; however, the fair market value of donated items must be reported as in-kind contributions and ticket sale proceeds must be reported as contributions.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: ocr; LLM: Document contains three related letters: original inquiry from Ms. Kearney (July 11, 1977), Secretary of State response (August 4, 1977) referring to FPPC, and FPPC response from Technical Assistance Program Coordinator. OCR quality is moderate with some scanning artifacts. Main FPPC advice letter clearly identifies Q/C structure.

embedding.summary: In response to a committee's inquiry about raffling donated quilts/afghans for fundraising, the FPPC confirms no prohibition exists but establishes reporting requirements: donated items must be reported at fair market value as in-kind contributions, and ticket sale proceeds must be reported as contributions.
